title: Has the oil price bubble burst? | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's Macro Oils team argues the recent Brent collapse reflects the burst of a war-risk bubble after the US-Iran MoU, not improved fundamentals; they forecast Brent averaging US$92/bbl in 2026 and US$78/bbl in 2027 (possibly US$70 by Q4 2027) but warn of a bumpy ride as the Strait of Hormuz reopening and 11mn b/d of shut-in supply slowly normalise.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts Brent averaging US$92/bbl in 2026 and US$78/bbl in 2027, potentially slipping to US$70/bbl by Q4 2027, assuming Strait of Hormuz transit flows normalise during August.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A concrete downward Brent path; positions for a multi-year oil-price decline contingent on August Hormuz normalisation.
insights.quote: The trend is down over the next 18 months with Brent averaging US$92/bbl in 2026 – buoyed by the elevated prices of March through May – and US$78/bbl in 2027 on our latest forecasts.
insights.locator: Where do Brent prices go from here?

insights.claim: The sharp drop in Brent is not justified by immediate fundamentals because very little additional oil has yet flowed through the Strait of Hormuz; the move reflects the binary repricing of war risk once reopening became likely.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: The price move is sentiment/positioning-driven, not supply-driven, so it is fragile and could reverse if negotiations stall.
insights.quote: It’s not justified on immediate fundamentals – very little additional oil has started to flow through the Strait based on our vessel tracking data.
insights.locator: Is the sharp drop in Brent price surprising?

insights.claim: In the four weeks to 16 June, investor positioning for higher Brent prices fell by around 80% from a five-year high that had held for much of the crisis.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Speculative length has been largely unwound, reducing further downside from positioning but limiting the cushion if supply disappoints.
insights.quote: investor positioning for higher Brent prices fell by around 80% from a five-year high that had held for much of the crisis
insights.locator: Is the sharp drop in Brent price surprising?

insights.claim: Had the Strait of Hormuz remained closed, Brent would have risen exponentially above US$150/bbl in the coming months; the prospect of reopening 'burst that bubble'.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Quantifies the tail risk still embedded if the MoU fails — a >US$150 spike — making the reopening assumption the pivotal swing factor.
insights.quote: Prolonged closure would lead to an exponential rise above US$150/bbl in the coming months; the prospect of reopening has burst that bubble.
insights.locator: Is the sharp drop in Brent price surprising?

insights.claim: The US-Iran MoU lays a foundation for a comprehensive agreement to be negotiated over 60 days, but negotiations could be extended or fail, and even if successful the future terms of transit through the Strait of Hormuz could differ greatly from the pre-war free flow of vessels.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Reopening is not binary-clean; constrained transit terms could keep a structural risk premium in oil even under a deal.
insights.quote: the future terms of transit through the Strait could be very different from the free flow of vessels before the war
insights.locator: What are the risks to the Strait of Hormuz reopening?

insights.claim: Iran's state press said the Strait will 'reopen under Iranian arrangements' and that it intends to limit transit to seven-hour weekday windows, signalling Iran believes it retains leverage despite the MoU.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Iranian-controlled, time-limited transit would cap throughput recovery and sustain volatility even after a nominal reopening.
insights.quote: Iran’s intention to limit transit to seven-hour weekday times shows it believes it still has leverage

insights.claim: Oil market fundamentals played an important role in reaching the MoU: with over 11 million b/d of crude production (plus 3 million b/d of refining capacity) shut in, accelerating drawdowns of commercial and strategic reserves were nearing a crisis point, with US Cushing inventories close to their operational floor.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: The deal was forced partly by an impending physical inventory crunch; explains why the US pushed hard and why a failure would be acute.
insights.quote: President Donald Trump acknowledged on 15 June that “we run out of reserves in about four weeks”
insights.locator: What part did oil market fundamentals play

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's May analysis estimates 70% of the 11mn-plus b/d of shut-in production could be back onstream within three months and 90% within six months, with the final ~1mn b/d taking considerably longer.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A staged supply-return curve to model the 2026-2027 oversupply; the slow last 1mn b/d sustains some tightness into 2027.
insights.quote: 70% of the 11 million b/d-plus of shut-in production could be back onstream within three months, and 90% within six months
insights.locator: The full value chain in the Gulf

insights.claim: Around 60 million barrels of oil held in vessels trapped in the Gulf will quickly reach consumer markets once the Strait is deemed safe, and Vessel Tracker data show ship transits rose from the low teens per day to a peak of 35 on 18 June, still well short of pre-war levels.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A 60mn-barrel overhang plus rising transits points to a near-term supply flush; track daily Hormuz transit counts as the real-time fundamental.
insights.quote: Around 60 million barrels of oil held in vessels trapped in the Gulf will quickly reach consumer markets once the Strait is deemed safe for transit.
insights.locator: Once the Strait has reopened, demand will surge

insights.claim: Outbound vessel transit recovers faster than inbound because trapped ships have a compelling reason to leave, whereas inbound shipowners must gamble that the Strait stays open long enough to re-emerge.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Asymmetric outbound/inbound risk means exports rebound before imports, shaping the sequence of the supply normalisation.
insights.quote: The risk calculus for outbound transit of vessels trapped for weeks is more compelling than that for inbound transit
insights.locator: How quickly could the market get back to ‘normal’?

insights.claim: Jet crack spreads, which hit a 'stratospheric' US$100/bbl when the Strait of Hormuz first closed, have eased but remain almost double pre-war levels, signalling lingering product-availability concerns and continued strong profitability for refiners able to deliver jet fuel.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Refiners with jet-fuel delivery capability keep 'printing money' while Gulf exports stay constrained; a near-term long-refining-margin signal.
insights.quote: refiners that can deliver jet fuel to customers while Gulf exports remain constrained will continue to print money
insights.locator: What are refining margins telling us?

insights.claim: Even if demand bounces back toward Wood Mackenzie's forecast 105 million b/d next year, the market should be well-supplied, with alternating periods of elevated and depressed prices as demand recovery, inventory build and supply return fall out of sync.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A well-supplied 2027 at ~105mn b/d demand favours range-trading and volatility over directional length; expect choppy, mean-reverting prices.
insights.quote: Even if demand bounces back towards our forecast 105 million b/d next year, the market should be well-supplied.
insights.locator: Where do Brent prices go from here?
